title: Folding Camp Stool
summary: An X-frame folding stool with a canvas sling seat — one bolt per side is the whole hinge, and it folds flat for the car.
description:
This is the classic director's-stool folding mechanism scaled down to a simple camp stool: two ladder-shaped frames, pinned together at a single bolt on each side, with a canvas sling that both makes the seat and locks the X open when you sit on it. There's no other hardware holding the fold shape — the tension of the fabric and your weight is what keeps it from collapsing, so the sling has to be sewn snug, not loose.

Build the two frames as near-identical twins (one is slightly wider so it nests inside the other when folded), predrill the pivot holes before assembly so they line up, and don't skip pre-finishing the wood before you attach the fabric — you can't sand around a sewn-in dowel afterward.
timeLabel: 2–3 hrs, plus sewing time for the sling

tools.note: You need a bit sized for the 1/4" pivot bolts and one for the 5/16" dowel holes.

materials.name: Canvas or heavy fabric for sling
materials.note: Needs to be sturdy — this is what you sit on and what holds the frame open.

cutList.note: Overhang 1/2" past the legs on each end.

steps.title: Build the two ladder frames
steps.body:
Build two identical-looking rectangular frames from legs, top rail, and stretchers — one uses the outer (wider) stretchers, the other the inner (narrower) ones, so the frames nest together when folded. Predrill and countersink for 2" screws, glue every joint, and keep the top rail overhanging 1/2" past the legs on each end.

Drill the 1/4" pivot hole through both legs of each frame now, at the same height on both, before you go further — it's much easier to line up on a flat frame than after everything is 3D.

steps.title: Pin the two frames together
steps.body: Lay the narrower frame inside the wider one so the predrilled pivot holes line up, then run a bolt through with a washer against each side of the joint and a nut on the end, finger-tight enough to pivot freely but not sloppy. Do this on both sides. This single bolt on each side is the entire folding hinge — it should swing smoothly with no binding.

steps.title: Sew the sling and dowel pockets
steps.body: Cut and hem a piece of canvas to the width of the top rails plus a few inches of wrap allowance, and sew a pocket at each end sized to slide over a dowel. The sling needs to fit snug enough that, once installed, it pulls the two frames taut against each other when you sit — a loose sling won't hold the stool open.
materials: Canvas or heavy fabric for sling

steps.title: Insert the dowels and finish
steps.body:
Slide the outer dowel through its sling pocket and set it into the outer frame, then the inner dowel into the inner frame the same way. Once both dowels are seated, the sling holds the whole X-frame under tension — the dowels stay hidden underneath the fabric.

Sand and finish the wood before this step if you haven't already; there's no going back once the sling is on.

images.alt: Folding wooden camp stool with a canvas sling seat
